title:
@victorialslocum: Effective 𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 isn't about how much you can store.

rawContent:
Tweet by @victorialslocum (Victoria Slocum)
Author bio: learning cool stuff, machine learning engineer at @weaviate_io 💙
Text: Effective 𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 isn't about how much you can store.

It's about what you choose to remember, how you organize it, and when you let it go.

Here are the four key principles we've learned for building agents with reliable memory:

𝟭. 𝗣𝗿𝘂𝗻𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗥𝗲𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗲
Memory needs regular maintenance. Periodically scan your long-term storage to remove duplicate entities, merge related information, and discard outdated facts. It's like cleaning out your closet - keep what you use all the time, combine similar items, toss what's old or not needed anymore.

𝟮. 𝗕𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲
Not every interaction deserves permanent storage. Implement a quality assessment mechanism before saving information to memory. This prevents your system from getting cluttered with low-value data that just adds noise during retrieval.

𝟯. 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗦𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝗥𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹
Always start with the simplest approach that works, then gradually layer in more advanced mechanisms as your use case demands it. Query augmentation, expanded queries, and reranking can all improve results - but only add complexity when you need it.

𝟰. 𝗧𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲
A simple similarity search often isn't enough. Advanced techniques like reranking and iterative retrieval can significantly improve the quality of retrieved information. Match your architecture to your specific task requirements.

The difference between an agentic system that works and one that works 𝘸𝘦𝘭𝘭 often comes down to how thoughtfully you manage memory, 𝘦𝘴𝘱𝘦𝘤𝘪𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘺 in a multiagent system. These principles apply whether you're building episodic memory for conversational agents or semantic memory for knowledge retrieval.

This is all part of context engineering - designing the systems that control what information reaches your model and how it maintains coherence over time.

Check out this ebook for more: https://t.co/5zMZFUnVXd
